coredata.dc:title: Surface modification of carbon dots with cyclodextrins as potential biocompatible photoluminescent delivery/bioimaging nanoplatform
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Rodríguez-Varillas, Sandra
Fontanil, Tania
Espina Casado, Jorge
Fernández-González, Alfonso
Badía Laíño, Rosana
coredata.dc:description:
Background Cyclodextrins are a well-established system which form inclusion complexes with many guest molecules. This property can be easily exploited to develop drug delivery systems. Additionally, carbon dots (CD) are a low-toxic photoluminescent product which have been used as luminescent tags. The combination of cyclodextrins and carbon dots allows obtaining a new nanoplatform, a biocompatible material, with both capabilities, increasing as well the internalization by the cells of the CD, induced by the cyclodextrins.
Results In the present work, we have modified the surface of carbon dots obtained from citric acid and glutathione with β and γ cyclodextrins. After a morphological and spectroscopic characterization, we concluded that the luminescence quantum yield and absorption molar coefficient of the derivatized and unmodified carbon dots was the same. These findings, together with the spectroscopic detection of active cyclodextrins, those bond to the CD able to interact with a guest molecule, allowed determination of the ratios: cyclodextrins/CD, active cyclodextrins/CD and an estimation of the CD molecular mass. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of the new materials was evaluated through cytotoxicity and cell-penetrance assays revealing that the materials were non cytotoxic up to 0.1 mg/mL. Moreover, the biocompatible developed nanoplatform penetrates in the cells maintaining the material's intrinsic fluorescence, thus constituting an adequate photoluminescent-tag with high-contrast for in vitro cell imaging.
Significance This work provides a new and easy method to combine cyclodextrins and carbon dots into a biocompatible material which can be used as nanoplatform both as drug delivery system and as photoluminescent tag in cell imaging. Likewise, this paper shows how to characterize the number of cyclodextrins and active cyclodextrins per CD, having an average stoichiometric relation of 1:1 for guest molecule – CD. Additionally, the minimum molecular mass of the unmodified CD was indirectly obtained, yielding about 1.6–1.9 kDa.

head.ce:title: Surface modification of carbon dots with cyclodextrins as potential biocompatible photoluminescent delivery/bioimaging nanoplatform

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cyclodextrins are a well-established system which form inclusion complexes with many guest molecules. This property can be easily exploited to develop drug delivery systems. Additionally, carbon dots (CD) are a low-toxic photoluminescent product which have been used as luminescent tags. The combination of cyclodextrins and carbon dots allows obtaining a new nanoplatform, a biocompatible material, with both capabilities, increasing as well the internalization by the cells of the CD, induced by the cyclodextrins.

In the present work, we have modified the surface of carbon dots obtained from citric acid and glutathione with β and γ cyclodextrins. After a morphological and spectroscopic characterization, we concluded that the luminescence quantum yield and absorption molar coefficient of the derivatized and unmodified carbon dots was the same. These findings, together with the spectroscopic detection of active cyclodextrins, those bond to the CD able to interact with a guest molecule, allowed determination of the ratios: cyclodextrins/CD, active cyclodextrins/CD and an estimation of the CD molecular mass. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of the new materials was evaluated through cytotoxicity and cell-penetrance assays revealing that the materials were non cytotoxic up to 0.1 mg/mL. Moreover, the biocompatible developed nanoplatform penetrates in the cells maintaining the material's intrinsic fluorescence, thus constituting an adequate photoluminescent-tag with high-contrast for in vitro cell imaging.

This work provides a new and easy method to combine cyclodextrins and carbon dots into a biocompatible material which can be used as nanoplatform both as drug delivery system and as photoluminescent tag in cell imaging. Likewise, this paper shows how to characterize the number of cyclodextrins and active cyclodextrins per CD, having an average stoichiometric relation of 1:1 for guest molecule – CD. Additionally, the minimum molecular mass of the unmodified CD was indirectly obtained, yielding about 1.6–1.9 kDa.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with functional groups on their surface containing heteroatoms such as H, N and O. They present a spherical shape with both crystalline and amorphous regions in their structures and a diameter usually below 10 nm [1]. Since their discovery in 2004 by Professor Scrievens [2], CDs have gained much attention due to their superior advantages such as excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, unique optoelectronic properties, resistance to photobleaching, high water solubilisation, low cost, and easy production al large-scale [3].

CDs can be synthesized by bottom-up and top-down approaches, leading to different characteristics of the final carbon material (size, shape, porosity, internal structure, optical properties, and so on). Bottom-up methods are the most common methods due to their simplicity, mild reaction conditions, and wide range of possible precursors. Among the variety of synthesis procedures, hydrothermal decomposition, based on dehydration and partial/total carbonization of the precursors, is widely used for its simplicity [4].

Obtaining CDs with different properties depends not only on the synthesis method used, but also on the starting carbon sources. Both natural and artificial precursor have been widely explored. This includes the use of small molecules (such as citric acid, ascorbic acid, chitosan or glucose), carbon materials (graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes, coal, etc.) and even waste products (favouring Green Chemistry). Starting materials can also be mixed with other molecules that enhance their properties, through surface passivation, heteroatom doping and/or functionalization. In particular, CDs doped with nitrogen (N) and/or sulphur (S) heteroatoms (also known as N/S-doped CDs), show improved fluorescence quantum yields, making them suitable for bioimaging and sensor purposes. For that approach, different molecules can be used, such as amines, urea, glutathione, ammonia, among other organic compounds.

Citric acid (CA) is one of the most widely used precursors in CDs synthesis, especially for those involving thermal decomposition at high temperatures [5]. This organic acid is naturally present in citrus fruits, and it is also added in food products and medicines due to its intrinsic properties. When used as carbon dots precursor, their high biocompatibility and biodegradability are transferred to the final material, together with remarkable antioxidant properties [6]. However, CA-based carbon dots may exhibit certain inconveniences, which can be overcome using a second molecule (mainly dopants or passivation agents) in combination with CA as carbon source.

Similar to citric acid, l-glutathione is an antioxidant, biocompatible, naturally present molecule in living organisms. The presence of carboxyl, amine, and thiol functional groups on its structure allow for interaction with citric acid during the synthesis process and provides the heteroatoms N and S, besides surface passivation. In addition to the above mentioned aspects, solvent type and reaction conditions (time and temperature) are also crucial with the aim of obtaining reproducible synthesis products, with stable physicochemical and optical properties between different batches [7].

The outstanding physicochemical and optical features of CDs make them suitable for a variety of fields such as biomedical, environmental, catalysis, solar energy, electrochemiluminescence sensors, optronic devices, and so on [8]. In cancer-related diseases, CDs can be used for treatment and diagnosis [9]. They can be loaded with therapeutic agents (like drugs and genes) and release them at the specific site of action due to Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect [10]. They can act as bioimaging agents because of their intrinsic fluorescence and absence of photobleaching [1]. Additional potential uses of CDs in the biomedical field include photodynamic and photothermal therapy [1] as well as antibacterial and antioxidant activity [8].

Surface modification is a key aspect of CDs enhancement. By this way, these fluorescent nanomaterials can be combined with other molecules or structures, improving their properties. The conjugation of supramolecular host molecules, such as crown ethers, cyclodextrins, calix[n]arenes, cucurbit[n]urils and pillar[n]arenes, improves retention capability and molecular recognition compared to free-CDs due to their cavities, in such a way that they can establish intermolecular interactions with analytes [11]. However, these modifications can alter the fluorescence properties of CDs.

Cyclodextrins (Cyd) are cyclic oligosaccharides of glucopyranose units linked with α-linkage, obtained from the degradation of starch with an enzyme called cyclodextrin glucanotransferase [12,13]. The most abundant Cyd in nature is formed by six, seven and eight glucopyranose units, denoted as α-cyclodextrin (α-Cyd), β-cyclodextrin (β-Cyd), and γ-cyclodextrin (γ-Cyd), respectively [14]. They are three-dimensional structures characterized by a hydrophilic exterior and a hydrophobic cavity capable of encapsulating hydrophobic molecules via host-guest inclusion complexes formation [15]. These complexes are formed through electrostatic interactions, van der Waals interactions, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophobic interactions between host and guest molecules [14]. By encapsulating guest molecules, the aqueous solubility of the guest is increased, and it is protected against oxidation, hydrolysis, dehydration, and photodecomposition [15]. Nevertheless, the retained compounds do not have to be idle in the cavity since they can undergo chemical reactions as well, which can be catalyzed by Cyd, acting then as an artificial enzyme [13]. Besides, based on Cyd ability to encapsulate molecules, different dyes have been used in order to quantify different types of cyclodextrins in several samples. The change in the spectroscopic properties of the encapsulated dye allows estimating the quantity of Cyd that is present. The most widely used coloured molecules for this purpose are methyl orange for α-Cyd quantification [16], phenolphthalein for samples containing β-Cyd [17] and bromocresol green (BCG) for those containing γ-Cyd [18].

Native Cyd have the GRAS status (Generally Recognized as Safe) for oral ingestion and they are approved by FDA for human use [19]. β-Cyd is the most commonly used Cyd in pharmaceutical products due to its solubilizing properties and low cost. Although α-Cyd presents a higher solubility than β-Cyd, its cavity is too small for various molecules. γ-Cyd has a superior safety profile, size and hydrophilicity. Moreover, its superior cavity size allows the incorporation of bigger molecules (such as steroids, proteins, etc.) in comparison to α-Cyd and β-Cyd. However, γ-Cyd is the most expensive Cyd [14].

Cyd and their derivatives find applications in various fields, including cosmetics, food, and pharmaceutical industries, among others [20,21]. Their extraordinary ability to encapsulate non-polar molecules and protect them against environmental factors, such as oxygen, light, or chemical reactions, has led to their commercial/industrial success [22]. For example, in the pharmaceutical industry, Cyd have been used to encapsulate various active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) to enhance their physicochemical and physiological stability in the physiological media. Additionally, they can interact with biological membranes to increase drug permeability and modulate the drug release at the target site [23]. Other applications include the removal of pollutants from air and water or their use as reducing agents for synthesis of metal nanoparticles [22].

As mentioned earlier, Cyd can be immobilized on CDs’ surface to improve their characteristics. By this way, encapsulation and selective recognition of analytes is enhanced by competitive host-guest interactions. Although Cyd generally do not quench CDs fluorescence, sometimes they block their fluorescence emission [11], affecting CDs quantum yield too. It is of particular interest the work of Espina-Casado et al., who linked β-Cyd to glutathione-based carbon dots. They reported that the β-Cyd had a marginal effect on CDs luminescence properties. However, the boronic groups used for binding the β-Cyd to the carbon dot were close enough to the surface to affect the electronic band structure, resulting in a decrease of about 30 % of the CDs luminescence quantum yield, from 0.49 to 0.35 [24].

In the pursuit of nanoplatforms suitable for biological applications, incorporating features such as protection, transport, controlled release, and accessibility to the cellular cytoplasm, along with the potential for bioimaging, this study employed a two pots green synthesis method to produce β/γ-Cyd-CDs. Initially, glutathione/citric acid-based CDs (UCDs) were synthesized using the traditional hydrothermal synthesis method, and subsequently, they were functionalized with carboxymethyl-Cyd (β-Cyd and γ-Cyd) through EDC·HCl/sulfo-NHS activation. The resulting materials were characterized according to their spectrophotometric and luminescent optical properties, size, shape, and surface functional groups. Moreover, the reaction of the Cyd linked CDs with bromocresol green and phenolphthalein, well-established probes for the quantification of cyclodextrins, allowed the investigation of the immobilized Cyd availability. Particularly, since the quantification is based on the interaction between the dye and the cyclodextrin, this methodology should not detect hindered cyclodextrins (either sterically or for other reasons) yielding, therefore, the amount of cyclodextrins that could encapsulate molecules in their cavities rather than the total amount of cyclodextrins present on the CDs.

Finally, biological studies on cell cultures were conducted to inquire about the penetrability and cytotoxicity capabilities of the synthesized material, revealing interesting features, such as good penetration while maintaining a low cytotoxic profile. The low cytotoxicity shown by the material, together with its high internalization by the cells depicts it as a promising luminescent tag for cell imaging.

l-Glutathione reduced (GSH), citric acid (CA), β-cyclodextrin (β-Cyd), γ-cyclodextrin (γ-Cyd), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), triethylamine, bromoacetic acid, sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) and bromocresol green (BCG) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 10x Dulbeco's Phosphate Buffered Saline (10x DPBS) and N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), methanol (MeOH) and absolute ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from VWR. Chloroform was acquired from Acros Organics, and phenolphthalein (Ph) from Panreac. Dialysis tubes (Pur-A-Lyzer,1 kDa MWCO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Cell proliferation was assessed using the Cell Titer 96 Non-Radioactive Cell Proliferation 91 Assay from Promega. Deionized Milli-Q water (MQW) was used throughout the experimental work.

Morphological and structural analysis of CDs and modified CDs were performed by High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) JEOL-JEM 2100F. The average diameter and the standard deviation were calculated using ImageJ software [25]. Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) spectra were collected on a Varian 670-IR Spectrometer in the range 600–4000 cm−1 at 4 cm−1 resolution and with 32 scans. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Single Differential Thermal Analysis (SDTA) were carried out using the Thermogravimetric Analyser TGA/SDTA851 from Mettler-Toledo coupled to Thermostar GSD301T Pfiffer-Vacuum Mass Spectrometer. Elemental Analysis were performed by C, H, N, S Analyzer Elementar Vario EL, using He as carrier gas and sulphadiazine as reference material for calibration. XPS measurements were carried out using a Phoibos hemispherical analyser, with monochromatized Kα Al radiation (1486.74 eV). High resolution spectra were taken using 30 eV energy pass and 0.1 eV energy step.

Absorption measurements of the samples were taken using a Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies, while excitation and emission luminescence spectra were obtained with a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer from Agilent Technologies. The quantum yields were determined using an FS5 Spectrofluorometer from Edinburgh Instruments and the calculations were performed by the instrument's Fluoracle® software.

Unmodified glutathione/citric acid-based CDs (UCDs) were synthesized according to the protocol described by J. Espina et al. [24]. Briefly, 2 g of CA and 1 g of GSH were dissolved in 10 mL of MQW and poured into a porcelain crucible. The mixture was heated in an oven at 180 °C for 2.5 h, adding 1 mL MQW every 30 min to avoid the product to scorch. After that, the mixture was let to cool down at room temperature and the orange-brownish gel obtained was dissolved in 10 mL of MQW. The final solution was purified by dialysis against MQW using dialysis tubes with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1 kDa for 20 h. The dialyzed solution was then vacuum concentrated in a rotary-evaporator, freeze-dried and the resulting orange solid, corresponding to GSH/CA CDs, was stored at 4 °C until further use.

5 g of β-Cyclodextrin (or 5.70 g γ-Cyclodextrin), 4.65 g of sodium hydroxide, 13.5 mL of bromoacetic acid (16 % v/v) and 16.5 mL MQW were mixed into a flask and heated at 50 °C during 5 h. The solution was neutralized with HCl solution (37 % v/v) and solvent was almost completely evaporated in a rotary-evaporator. The product was precipitated by adding 20 mL of pure methanol, as carboxymethyl-cyclodextrins (CM-Cyd) are insoluble in this solvent. The solid was separated by Büchner filtration, washing several times with cool methanol. Finally, the white powder was vacuum dried and kept at room temperature until further use.

In a two-necked flask under vacuum, 0.1g sulfo-NHS, 0.2 g EDC·HCl and 0.40 g CM-Cyd were added. Then, system was saturated with N2. 15 mL of previously deoxygenated MQW were added, and the mixture was kept under magnetic stirring for 1 h. Afterward, 15 mL of MQW and 0.20 g of UCDs were added to the reaction mixture. Finally, 5 μL of triethylamine were added, and the mixture was left to react for 24 h, keeping the system saturated with N2. The solution obtained was purified through liquid-liquid extraction (three times, with 10 mL of chloroform each), followed by overnight dialysis. Subsequently, the solvent volume was reduced in a rotary-evaporator and the final product is freeze-dried.

The concentration of β-Cyd was calculated following the method described by A. Goel et al. [17] with some modifications. Briefly, 50 μL of a 4 mM phenolphthalein (Ph) working solution in absolute EtOH were mixed with different volumes of β-Cyd stock solution (5 mg/mL) up to a final volume of 5 mL with carbonate buffer 0.03 M (pH = 10.4). Likewise,100 μL of 10 mg/mL β -Cyd-CDs solution was added over Ph to determine β-Cyd concentration following the same procedure. Samples were evaluated in triplicate.

The γ-Cyd concentration was determined following the method described by T. Kato et al. [18] with some modifications. Briefly, 400 μL of a 0.5 mmol/L bromocresol green (BCG) working solution in EtOH 20 % (v/v) are mixed with different volumes of γ-Cyd stock solution (6.5 mg/mL) up to a final volume of 5 mL with citrate buffer 0.05 M (pH = 4.2). The calibration curve was performed in the range 0–250 μg/mL γ-Cyd. In order to determine γ-Cyd in γ-Cyd-CDs, 250 μL of 10 mg/mL γ-Cyd-CDs solution were added over BCG following the same protocol. Samples were evaluated in triplicate.

Assays were conducted using the CellTiter 96® Non-Radioactive Cell Proliferation Assay kit from Promega according to the vendor instructions. Human dermal fibroblasts and A549 (lung carcinoma) cells were seeded at a concentration 5000 cells per well in a 96-well plate. All cells were cultured in an incubator at 37 °C with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere, using DMEM medium (10 % heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum) supplemented with 100 U/mL penicillin and 50 μg/mL streptomycin. Cells were exposed to CDs at concentrations up to 1 mg mL−1 for 5 days. Yellow MTT salt crystals were added to the cells daily throughout the experiment, and formazan absorbance was measured as described below.

The cytotoxicity of the products was evaluated through a cell proliferation assay, also known as MTT assay, over a period of five days. The procedure is based on determining the activity of the mitochondrial succinic dehydrogenase enzyme. This activity is evaluated through the conversion of yellow MTT into insoluble blue formazan crystals ((E,Z)-5-(4.5 dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-1,3-difenilformazan) and measuring the formazan formation through the absorbance at 570 nm.

Next, cell viability is assessed by comparing it with its initial absorbance:

Where A x h is the average absorption of the sample at different time points, and A 0 h is the average absorption of the cells on the initial day of the experiment.

In our assay, different concentrations of the materials to be tested (0.1 and 1 mg/mL) are brought into contact with healthy human dermal fibroblasts and lung cancer cells (A549 cell line). Cells are cultured and kept under the aforementioned controlled conditions.

Cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 105 on coverslips placed in 24-well plates (Menzel-Gläser, Germany) and allowed to adhere for 24 h under standard conditions. Subsequently, the cells were incubated in the presence of a 1 mg/ml solution of β-CyD-CDs or γ-Cyd-CDs in the culture medium for 60 min, maintaining the same conditions. Afterward, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed three times with PBS 1X pH 7.4. Finally, the cells were fixed with 4 % formaldehyde and mounted using a Vectashield Antifade mounting medium. In conclusion, images were captured using a Leica SP8 confocal microscope.

A549 cells (lung carcinoma) and Human Dermal Fibroblasts were seeded in a 96-well plate at a concentration of 5000 cells/well. The cells were exposed for 1 h to different amounts of each type of CDs, ensuring that the initial fluorescence intensity of the solutions was consistent across all experiments for the different nanomaterials tested. Subsequently, the culture medium was removed, and the sample were washed three times with 1X PBS. The fluorescence intensity was measured using a Biotek Hybrid Synergy H4 plate fluorescence reader with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength of 430 nm. The experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the control refers to the fluorescence emitted by cells without CDs.

The developed nanomaterials, β/γ-Cyd-CD, were obtained through a two-pot synthesis following the steps outlined in Scheme 1 . In this scheme, unmodified glutathione/citric-acid based CDs (UCDs) were first synthesized using the traditional hydrothermal method (step 1) and subsequently functionalized with carboxymethyl-Cyd (β-Cyd and γ-Cyd) through activation with EDC·HCl/sulfo-NHS. Carboxymethyl-Cyd were previously obtained from commercial cyclodextrins using bromoacetic acid as the reagent (step 0). The different products of synthesis were characterized using ATR-FTIR and mass spectrometry (Spectra are shown in Supplementary Information, Figures SI1 and SI2). CDs obtained from citric acid and glutathione exhibited a spectrum consistent with that of similar nanoparticles already published [26]. The most interesting feature in the CM-β-Cyd and CM-γ-Cyd FTIR spectra lies in the range 1500–1750 cm−1 (figure SI1). β-Cyd shown a peak at 1641 cm−1 which is assigned to O–H bending H2O [27]. This peak is still present in CM-β-Cyd as a shoulder, and it is overlapped by an intense peak at 1595 cm−1 belonging to the introduced carboxylic group [28]. A similar situation is observed in the case of the γ-Cyd with the O–H bending H2O at 1637 cm−1, which still appears in the CM-γ-Cyd as a shoulder of the intense carboxylic peak at 1593 cm−1. The presence of the new carboxylic group confirms the carboxymethylation of both types of cyclodextrins.

On the other hand, the C–O stretching of primary and secondary hydroxyl groups appears respectively at 1020 and 1076 cm−1 both in β-Cyd and CM-β-Cyd [27]. These two peaks are also present in β-Cyd-CDs at 1026 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1, thus confirming the presence of cyclodextrins in the material. Similarly, primary and secondary hydroxyl groups in γ-Cyd appear at 1018 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 respectively and at 1018 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 in the case of CM-γ-Cyd. The peaks remain almost in the same position in the γ-Cyd-CDs structure, appearing at 1024 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1. We can, therefore, confidently conclude that cyclodextrins were properly carboxymethylated and that the carboxymethylated oligosaccharides were correctly bond to the carbon dot surface.

In addition to the FTIR results, mass spectrometry results supported the same conclusion too. The mass spectrum of CM-β-Cyd (Figure SI2) shows peaks at 1157.36 Da, 1215.36 Da, 1273.36 Da and a small one at 1331.37 Da. These values correspond to the molecular weights of Na-β-Cyd, Na-β-Cyd–(CH2–CO2 -), Na- β-Cyd-Na–(CH2–CO2 -)2 and β-Cyd–(CH2–CO2 -)3 respectively, thus confirming the carboxylation of β-Cyd. Likewise, the mass spectrum of CM-γ-Cyd shows peaks at 1297.41 Da, 1319.39 Da, 1355.41 Da, 1377.39 Da and a very small peak at 1435.40 Da which correspond with the molecular weights of γ-Cyd, Na- γ-Cyd, γ-Cyd-CO2H, γ-Cyd-CO2Na, γ-Cyd-Na-(CO2H)2 respectively. Again, the carboxymethylation of γ-Cyd is confirmed.

HR-TEM images confirmed that unmodified CDs (UCDs) and those modified (β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs) had a spherical shape with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 1 ) and diameters in the range of 3.0–3.6 nm (SD < 0.7 nm). Lattice spacing close to 0.24 nm (see Table SI1) suggests a graphitic structure, consistent with that of graphene [1 0 0] planes [29]. Thus, modification with CM-β/γ-Cyd did not significantly alter either the shape or the dimensions of de CDs.

TGA and SDTA analysis were carried out to establish the thermal stability of CDs before and after modification with cyclodextrins. In all cases, the thermogram showed a three-step degradation pattern. Initially, UCDs, β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs showed all a weight loss in the range 25 °C – 150 °C caused by the evaporation of water molecules. This weight loss was of 3.1 %, 5.1 % and 6 % respectively. This increase of weight loss in both Cyd-modified CDs compared to the UCDs is consistent with the presence of water molecules within the hydrophobic cavity of cyclodextrins [30]. In the second stage, ranging from 150 °C to 370 °C, a mass loss of 54.0 % (UCDs), 41.7 % (β-Cyd-CDs), and 33.5 % (γ-Cyd-CDs) was observed, along with the release of CO and CO2. This high loss may be attributed to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups (CO and –COOH) and to the deamination of nitrogen moieties [31,32]. Furthermore, β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs showed a very intense exothermic process in this interval due to the loss of CO2 from cyclodextrins attached to CDs surface. Finally, a very slow mass loss observed in the range 370 °C to 1000 °C for the three types of materials which arises from the decomposition of the carbon core of CDs [31]. After calcination was completed at 1000 °C, no residue was left.

Additionally, UCD and γ-Cyd-CD underwent elemental analysis and XPS, whose results are summarized in Table 1 . The elemental analysis results reveal a 1:2 ratio of S:N, consistent with the S:N ratio in glutathione, which serves as the unique source of those elements in CD. Moreover, the higher amount of C and H in γ-Cyd-CD compared to UCD are consistent with the presence of cyclodextrins surrounding the UCD surface.

The molar ratio N:S obtained from XPS is comparable to that obtained from elemental analysis. Carbon ratio is not estimated in XPS, due to the uncontrollable presence of spurious carbon. γ-Cyd-CD also exhibit a small amount of Na (molar ratio Na:S ca. 0.23) attributed to an undesired reaction between N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt and some surface carboxyl groups of UCDs [38]. High resolution spectra for C, O and S were taken too. It is important to mention that the curve fitting for C1s is not a straightforward task which, in complex samples like ours, where carbon may be bonded to different functionalities and in different chemical environments, becomes particularly delicate [33]. Therefore, we attempted a preliminary analysis by comparing the C1s spectra in UCD and γ-Cyd-CD (figure SI3 a). The primary distinction in both spectra is the banishment of the peak located around 288.8 eV after the modification of the UCD. High binding energies are normally related to high oxidation states, which in this case could involve carboxylic moieties. The banishment of this peak may be attributed to the presence of bulky cyclodextrins on the carbon dot surface, which masks the presence of the underlying functionalities. This is further supported by the observed increase in the intensity of the medium-range binding energies (around 286.2 eV) in the γ-Cyd-CD sample, where C–O bonds from cyclodextrins are expected to appear.

Another notable feature observed in the XPS spectra is the presence of two S2p doublets in γ-Cyd-CD (figure SI3 b) whereas only a single doublet appears in UCD. This is easily explained considering the presence of a single type of S atom in UCDs, originating from glutathione, which can be assigned to –SH [34]. In modified samples, two types of S atoms appear, one similar to that in non-functionalized UCDs, and another potentially assigned to a sulfonic group, confirming the presence of N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide in the modified nanoparticle.

Regarding the O1s spectra (Figure SI3 c), UCD show a main peak with a shoulder which appear around 531.9 eV and 533.3 eV respectively, belonging probably to C–O and CO functionalities from carboxylic groups [35]. γ-Cyd-CD appears to be a single peak centred at 532.3 eV, or a combination of an intense peak at 532.4 eV and a smaller one around 531.3 eV. In either case, the presence of an intense band at the 532.3 eV position is indisputable and fully consistent with the presence of C–O bonds in cyclodextrins [36].

The UV–Vis absorption profiles of UCDs, β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The absorption spectrum of UCDs is consistent with the expected for graphene-based Carbon Dots, exhibiting a typical absorption band at 346 nm attributed to n-π* transitions from CO bonds and a slight shoulder around 240 nm ascribed to π -π* transitions from C–C bonds with sp2 hybridization [37]. The absorption spectra of modified CDs (both with CM-β-Cyd and CM-γ-Cyd) show a similar profile to that of UCDs. This fact is due to β-Cyd, γ-Cyd, CM-β-Cyd and CM-γ-Cyd do not exhibit spectrophotometric or luminescent properties per se.

Photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of the synthetized CDs and modified-CDs are shown in Fig. 3 . CDs are characterized by an intense emission peak centred at 422 nm under an excitation of 346 nm, which are consistent with the luminescent wavelengths of other already published carbon dots [24,26,38]. Modification with CM-β-Cyd and CM-γ-Cyd does not lead to any shift in the maximum excitation or emission wavelength, although the intensity is lower for the same mass concentration studied (10−3 mg/mL). This decrease is a direct consequence of the molecular weight, as for the same weight of product, β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs have less quantity of CDs than the sample that is only composed of UCDs, which is in fact the fluorophore of the platform. This difference becomes particularly evident when characterizing the quantum yield of the different materials, obtaining values of 65 ± 3 % for UCDs, 66 ± 3 % for β-Cyd-CDs and 65 ± 3 % for γ-Cyd-CDs. Deoxygenation of samples did not modify significantly the quantum yield values. It is clear, then, that the presence of CM-Cyd does not modify luminescent properties of CDs.

Furthermore, these high quantum yield values are consistent with those reported for other similar carbon dots (57 % for GSH and CA based CD [26], 56 % for CD from 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid and glycine [39] or 51 % for β-Cyd-CD [24]). The most accepted explanation for these high quantum yields is the existence of quantum confinements of emissive energy trap on the nanoparticle surface, which becomes highly emissive upon surface passivation [40,41]. In fact, different surface patches on the same carbon dot may contribute differently to its photoluminescence, resulting in different overlapping bands with different behaviour with metal ions [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of efficiency of CDs functionalization
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the quantity of β-Cyd and γ-Cyd in β-Cyd-CD and γ-Cyd-CD, colorimetric methods using phenolphthalein and bromocresol green were employed, respectively, following the protocol previously described in section 2.6. Briefly, both methods are based on the modification of the spectroscopic characteristics of the dye after the formation of the inclusion complex: a hypochromic effect in the absorption band at 553 nm for the β-Cyd: phenolphthalein system and a hyperchromic effect for the γ-Cyd: bromocresol green complex (Figure SI4).

Under the experimental conditions selected, calibration curves were obtained in the range 0–100 μg/mL for β-Cyd (λ = 553 nm) and 0–250 μg/mL for γ-Cyd (λ = 625 nm) (see Figure SI5). The obtained calibration curve were:
Abs (a.u.) = (−2.4 ± 0.1)·[ β-Cyd] (mg/mL) + (0.445 ± 0.008) for β-Cyd
and
Abs (a.u.) = (0.36 ± 0.02)·[ γ-Cyd] (mg/mL) + (0.184 ± 0.002) for γ-Cyd
The analytical figures of merit, including limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), linearity, sensitivity and determination coefficient, are shown in Table SI2 for both methods.

Calibration graphs exhibited fairly good linear correlative relationships (R2 = 0.990 for β-Cyd calibration curve and R2 = 0.993 for the γ-Cyd one) between Cyd concentrations and response in terms of absorbance intensity.

Calibration graphs had a fairly good linear correlative relationships (R2 = 0.990 for β-Cyd calibration curve and R2 = 0.993 for the γ-Cyd one) between Cyd concentrations and response in terms of absorbance intensity. LOD and LOQ were determined according to 3·Sb/slope and 10·Sb/slope (where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank), respectively, following IUPAC recommendations. Sensitivity is the slope of the calibration curve, and the obtained LOD and LOQ are suitable for detecting and quantifying both β-Cyd and γ-Cyd in samples.

By this way and based on external calibration curves, it was found that the amount of β-Cyd in β-Cyd-CDs was 40.9 ± 0.3 % (w/w) while it was 39 ± 2 % (w/w) γ-Cyd in γ-Cyd-CDs. It is important to note that these percentages correspond to the amount of cyclodextrin that is accessible for interaction with chromophores, not to the total cyclodextrin present in modified structures, where accessibility may be limited by steric factors or the formation of Cyd2-dye type complexes [42].

On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the quantum yield, absorption molar coefficient and the concentration [43]:

However, this percentage is not consistent with those obtained in the absorption experiments, which yielded 40.9 ± 0.3 % (w/w) for β-Cyd-CD and 39 ± 2 % (w/w) in the case of γ-Cyd-CD. The discrepancy in explained considering that only a few of the surface-attached cyclodextrins is available for encapsulating the dye and, therefore, is detectable in spectrophotometric experiments.

Bearing this in mind, we calculated the mass percentage of cyclodextrins in the Cyd-CD system obtaining the molecular mass with nactual cyclodextrins but assuming the mass of only n active cyclodextrins, with nactive < nactual. Equations [5–7] are modified accordingly:

Where r is the nactive/nactual ratio, resulting in rβ = 0.163 and rγ = 0.133. Table SI4 records the weight percentage of cyclodextrin for different values of nactive and nactual, with respective values of 1 and 6 for β-Cyd-CD and 1 and 7 for γ-Cyd-CD.

Taking into account the different possible molecular weights of UCD (which depend on nactual, equation [4]), we estimated the stoichiometric ratio Cyd:CD present during the synthesis, finding that it ranges from 0.5 to ca. 20 (Table SI5). Thus, an intermediate situation, where a maximum of 10 cyclodextrins are attached to a single CD is assumed, resulting in a likely molecular weight of 2763 g mol−1 (data from β-Cyd) or 2790 g mol−1 (data from γ-Cyd).

Thus, the combined treatment of data provided by spectrophotometric assays, based on the formation of an inclusion complex, and intrinsic fluorescence of the CDs complement each other, allowing the assessment not only of the estimated molecular mass of the UCDs but also the average number of immobilized cyclodextrins and the stoichiometric ratio of these capable of interacting with the dye. The developed procedure would serve as a starting point to employ this methodology, not only for assessing the efficiency of functionalization but also for exploring its potential as a photoluminescent labeled-drug delivery platform for active principles whose inclusion complexes with cyclodextrins exhibit quantifiable changes in their absorption spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the media and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on the photoluminescence emission of CDs was evaluated using a 10−3 mg L−1 CDs solution in phosphate buffer across a pH range of 4–8, using λExc = 346 nm and λEm = 422 nm (Figure SI6a). All samples show a stable signal independent of pH levels, although with a very slight increase in the photoluminescence of β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs at pH 7. The origin of this behaviour is uncertain and it does not seem to be related to the pKa of citric acid (3.09, 4.75, 5.41 [26]) or glutathione (2.12, 3.53, 8.66, 9.62 [44]), nor with the UCDs themselves [26], but rather to the presence of cyclodextrins. The nature of the buffering solution was also tested by checking the photoluminescence at pH 6 using citrate, phosphate, phthalate or maleate, without finding significant changes in the emission intensity or wavelength (Figure SI6b).

Concerning the temperature effect, the luminescence intensity decreases with temperature, as expected. Figure SI7 a shows the variation of fluorescence emission with temperature for UCD, β-Cyd-CD and γ-Cyd-CD at concentrations 1 mg mL−1, following in all cases a linear trend in the way IT = IT0-KT·T. From the previous discussion, it is obvious that the lower fluorescence intensity detected in β-Cyd-CD and γ-Cyd-CD in these assays comes from the fact that the molar concentration of them is lower than the molar concentration of UCD at the same mass concentration of 1 mg mL−1. Considering the linear trend and equation [3] we can write:
[11]
I T UCD

Where equation [12] can be written identically for β-Cyd-CD. The representation of the fluorescence normalized to the lowest measured temperature rather than the fluorescence itself, produces therefore identical curves for the three nanomaterials (Figure SI6b). Table SI6 summarizes the temperature effect.

Cell proliferation studies, assessed through cellular mitochondrial activity using the MTT assay, were conducted by exposing A549 cell lines and human dermal fibroblasts to different concentrations of nanoparticles, specifically, 0.1 and 1.0 mg/mL for 96 h. Fig. 4 shows the proliferation assay for the different materials in both cell lines. Neither the individual CDs nor the Cyd-CDs show different behaviour compared to the control culture at a concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 in both cell lines, suggesting that such concentration is not cytotoxic, making it a safe concentration for use. When the concentration is raised to 1 mg mL−1, the proliferation of the cells exposed to the materials is smaller than the control, indicating a higher cytotoxicity and this difference becomes more pronounced with longer exposure times. However, it is important to note that after 24 h, cell viability at our highest concentration (1 mg/ml) was 75 % for UCDs, 80 % for β-Cyd-CDs, and 90 % for γ-Cyd-CDs, results comparable to those obtained in other studies such as that of Yang and colleagues in 2019 [45], who examined CDs functionalized with β-Cyclodextrin in HeLa tumor cells and healthy HEK 293 T cells. This fact becomes particularly relevant taking into account the use of immortalized cells done by Yang et al. rather than non-immortalized healthy dermal fibroblasts as we do, what provides a more accurate representation of healthy cells.

It is noteworthy that within the studied concentration range, no significant differences were observed in cell-induced toxicity between the different CDs analysed in the two cell lines. Several studies suggest that CD cytotoxicity may be attributed to photodegraded products generated over time, temperature, pH, light intensity, and wavelength variations [46,47]. Importantly, this toxicity equally affects both healthy and tumor cells [47].

Fig. 5 .a and 5.b depict cellular fluorescence intensity following exposure to varying concentrations of CDs. The control fluorescence signal represents cellular autofluorescence. According to these results, cells internalize both single carbon dots and the two versions derivatized with beta and gamma cyclodextrins. However, for an accurate interpretation of the data, it is crucial to consider that UCDs exhibit fluorescence 3.5 times greater fluorescence than β-Cyd-CDs and 10 times greater than γ-Cyd-CDs at equivalent concentrations. With this correction, it is established that cyclodextrin-derivatized CDs are internalized to the same extent as UCDs. As an example, Fig. 5.c provides confocal fluorescence microscope images of the A549 cell line before and after internalization of β-Cyd-CDs and γ-Cyd-CDs, where it can be seen that the nanomaterial penetrates and accumulates as discrete points in the cytoplasm but not the nucleus of the cell, while maintaining its intrinsic fluorescence. In this way, the synthesized material is presented as an adequate photoluminescent-labeled-system with high-contrast for in vitro imaging that allows for the monitoring of the movement and destination of active principles that act as guest inside cyclodextrins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proof of concept: loading γ-Cyd-CDs with pharmaceuticals
ce:section.ce:para: In light of current bibliographic evidence, it is indisputable that the use of supramolecular host molecules provides effective systems for designing controlled drug release systems. These pharmacological delivery systems are attractive in a wide range of therapies spanning from treating inflammatory or infectious processes, pain management to cancer, among others. Integrating these systems into nanoplatforms such as the developed nanomaterials would not only allow for the transport/controlled release of drugs but also provide added value due to certain intrinsic properties of CDs (antioxidant action, penetrability, luminescence, dispersibility, among others), enhancing their therapeutic action.

To assess whether the developed nanoplatforms exhibit favorable behavior as a transport system, preliminary loading tests were conducted using various model drugs: a) an analgesic, acetylsalicylic acid, b) two antibiotic such as chlortetracycline and nafcillin, and c) an anticoagulant, warfarin. For all these pharmaceutical models but nafcillin, it has been reported in the literature that they form inclusion complexes with cyclodextrins [48–51]. Nonetheless, the 2-ethoxynaphtyl moiety present in nafcillin resembles the neroline, whose encapsulation in cyclodextrines has been exploited in cosmetic and textile industries [52,53]. However, the ionization of the drug molecules greatly reduces the formation of such complexes and improves solubility in aqueous medium. Therefore, methanol was chosen as the charging medium, serving as a compromise solvent to allow both a proper solubilisation of the drug to be loaded and an adequate medium to form the inclusion complex. Only γ-Cyd-CDs were utilized, as they possess cyclodextrins with the highest capacity to host the guest molecules. For comparative purposes, the same protocol was employed in all cases: 5 mg of γ-Cyd-CDs (4.49·10−7 mols) were weighed into an Eppendorf tube and brought into contact with 1 mL of a 2.00·10−3 M drug solution in methanol. The mixture was then subjected to orbital agitation, kept in darkness, and maintained at room temperature for 18 h. Subsequently, it was centrifuged at 5300 rpm for 25 min to separate the supernatant. The free drug in the supernatant was quantified using a spectrophotometric method, and the retained amount was obtained by difference.

Table 2 summarizes the obtained results. For the sake of simplicity, the percentage of drug retained in the γ-Cyd-CDs was calculated considering the molecular weight of CDs functionalized with seven γ-Cyds, of which only one has loading capacity, meaning a 1:1 ratio between the γ-Cyd-CDs and the drug.

It is important to state that these calculations are not affected by the actual molecular mass of the carbon dots. The consideration of 2 active cyclodextrins with 14 actual cyclodextrins around a single carbon dot would render the molecular mass also twice, keeping the molar ratio constant.

Results in Table 2 show that, despite needing a proper optimization of the encapsulation procedure for every drug, our nanoplatform exhibits a promising performance in retaining pharmaceutical drugs. In fact, nafcillin shows the second best result in spite of not being reported previously its inclusion complex with cyclodextrin. Thus, the developed nanoplatforms, consisting of cyclodextrins and CDs, emerge as highly promising materials that can enhance efficacy and/or reduce side effects. This is partly attributed to a more controlled dosage of the drug at the target tissue, coupled with the possibility of tracking its internalization via fluorescence and its antioxidative action.

In this study, carbon nanoparticles based on glutathione have been successfully functionalized with cyclodextrins, presenting a multifunctional platform. In addition to serving as a cellular glucose source, this functionalization can act as a potential controlled transport and delivery system for active compounds. Spectrophotometric studies using dyes as model inclusion analytes in the Cyds, combined with the observation that functionalization does not affect the fluorescence quantum efficiency of the CDs, facilitated the indirect determination of the molecular mass of the employed nonfunctionalized carbon nanoparticles, the number of linked Cyds, and an estimated average stoichiometric relation of 1:1 for guest molecule – CD complex with respect to the functionalized nanoparticle. On the other hand, cytotoxicity assessments conducted on two distinct cell lines, fibroblast and lung carcinoma cells, revealed no toxicity of the Cyd-CDs at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL−1. Moreover, fluorescence imaging demonstrated that Cyd-CDs not only penetrate the interior of cells and accumulate as discrete points in the cytoplasm but also maintain their intrinsic fluorescence, enabling high-contrast in vitro imaging. Additionally, the cell penetration of Cyd-CDs is higher than that of UCDs. These results suggest that these materials are a promising alternative for bioimaging applications.

Overall, our results highlight that the synthesized Cyd-CDs show great potential for use as drug carriers and releasers. Moreover, the developed methodology allows estimation, for active principles whose optical absorption is modified upon forming inclusion complexes with cyclodextrins, of the stoichiometric relationship with CDs.
